edges.description: Disrupted foregut developmental signaling can impair normal partitioning of respiratory and esophageal lineages.
edges.source: Developmental signaling defects in foregut morphogenesis

edges.description: Failed compartmentalization can leave esophageal discontinuity with persistent tracheoesophageal communication.
edges.target: Interrupted esophageal continuity and tracheoesophageal communication

edges.description: Esophageal discontinuity and fistulous airway communication produce inability to swallow, retained secretions, and aspiration risk.
edges.source: Interrupted esophageal continuity and tracheoesophageal communication
edges.target: Neonatal feeding obstruction and aspiration risk

edges.description: The congenital esophageal lesion and its surgical repair create the clinical context in which long-term dysmotility and aerodigestive morbidity are observed.
edges.source: Interrupted esophageal continuity and tracheoesophageal communication
edges.target: Post-repair esophageal dysmotility and aerodigestive morbidity

Primary surgical repair with tracheoesophageal fistula ligation
Balloon dilatation for post-repair anastomotic strictures

nodes.description: Anastomotic strictures after esophageal atresia repair are often managed with fluoroscopic or endoscopic balloon dilatation.
nodes.id: Balloon dilatation for post-repair anastomotic strictures

evidence.reference_title: Balloon dilatation of anastomotic strictures secondary to surgical repair of oesophageal atresia: a systematic review.
evidence.snippet: These strictures are often treated by balloon dilatation (BD) and currently balloon dilatation (fluoroscopic or endoscopic) is the preferred primary treatment method.

nodes.description: Human syndromic observations and animal models implicate disrupted foregut developmental signaling, including sonic hedgehog pathway effectors, in the susceptibility to esophageal atresia and tracheoesophageal fistula.
nodes.id: Developmental signaling defects in foregut morphogenesis

evidence.reference_title: Etiology of esophageal atresia and tracheoesophageal fistula: "mind the gap".
evidence.snippet: Studies in animal models implicate an essential role for sonic hedgehog signaling and its downstream effectors in the correct embryogenesis of the foregut.

evidence.reference_title: Genetic Mouse Models and Induced Pluripotent Stem Cells for Studying Tracheal-Esophageal Separation and Esophageal Development.
evidence.snippet: animal models have shown the involvement of multiple signaling pathways and transcription factors in the development of the esophagus and trachea.

nodes.description: Swallowing difficulty is a frequent clinical problem before and after repair.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002015 | Dysphagia | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.reference_title: ESPGHAN-NASPGHAN Guidelines for the Evaluation and Treatment of Gastrointestinal and Nutritional Complications in Children With Esophageal Atresia-Tracheoesophageal Fistula.
evidence.snippet: investigation and management of dysphagia

nodes.description: Interruption of esophageal continuity is the defining malformation.

evidence.snippet: interruption in the continuity of the esophagus
congenital abnormality of the oesophagus

nodes.description: GERD is a recognized post-repair gastrointestinal complication.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002020 | Gastroesophageal reflux | Occasional (29-5%)

evidence.reference_title: ESPGHAN-NASPGHAN Guidelines for the Evaluation and Treatment of Gastrointestinal and Nutritional Complications in Children With Esophageal Atresia-Tracheoesophageal Fistula.
evidence.snippet: diagnosis, and treatment of gastroesophageal reflux

nodes.description: Long-term care includes treatment of gastroesophageal reflux after EA/TEF repair.

evidence.reference_title: ESPGHAN-NASPGHAN Guidelines for the Evaluation and Treatment of Gastrointestinal and Nutritional Complications in Children With Esophageal Atresia-Tracheoesophageal Fistula.
evidence.snippet: Questions on the diagnosis, and treatment of gastroesophageal reflux

nodes.description: The anterior foregut normally partitions into ventral respiratory and dorsal esophageal compartments; incomplete embryonic compartmentalization produces the developmental context for esophageal interruption and persistent airway communication.

evidence.snippet: Oesophageal atresia (EA) is a congenital abnormality of the oesophagus that is caused by incomplete embryonic compartmentalization of the foregut.

evidence.reference_title: Genetic Mouse Models and Induced Pluripotent Stem Cells for Studying Tracheal-Esophageal Separation and Esophageal Development.
evidence.snippet: Esophagus and trachea arise from a common origin, the anterior foregut tube.

nodes.description: The defining anatomic outcome is interruption of esophageal continuity, with or without a persistent fistulous connection between the trachea and esophagus.
nodes.id: Interrupted esophageal continuity and tracheoesophageal communication

evidence.snippet: an interruption in the continuity of the esophagus, with or without persistent communication with the trachea
EA commonly occurs with a tracheo-oesophageal fistula (TEF).

nodes.description: Because swallowed material cannot pass normally to the stomach and may communicate with the airway, neonates develop feeding difficulty, excessive secretions, vomiting or choking, respiratory distress, and aspiration complications.
nodes.id: Neonatal feeding obstruction and aspiration risk

evidence.snippet: can lead to the inability to swallow or, in the most severe cases, respiratory distress.

evidence.reference_title: Appearances are Deceptive - Passing a Nasogastric Tube does Not Always Rule Out Oesophageal Atresia.
evidence.snippet: inability to pass a nasogastric tube (NGT)

nodes.description: Even after anatomic repair, esophageal dysmotility and reflux-related complications contribute to ongoing gastrointestinal and respiratory morbidity.
nodes.id: Post-repair esophageal dysmotility and aerodigestive morbidity

evidence.snippet: Oesophageal motility disorders are probably ubiquitous in patients after undergoing EA repair

evidence.reference_title: ESPGHAN-NASPGHAN Guidelines for the Evaluation and Treatment of Gastrointestinal and Nutritional Complications in Children With Esophageal Atresia-Tracheoesophageal Fistula.
evidence.snippet: These children face gastrointestinal (GI) problems not only in early childhood but also through adolescence and adulthood.

nodes.description: Standard neonatal management restores esophageal continuity and ligates or divides a tracheoesophageal fistula when present.
nodes.id: Primary surgical repair with tracheoesophageal fistula ligation

evidence.snippet: EA is treated surgically to restore the oesophageal interruption and, if present, ligate and divide the TEF.

evidence.reference_title: Perioperative management and outcomes of esophageal atresia and tracheoesophageal fistula.
evidence.snippet: surgical repair of their defect defined as esophageal reconstruction with or without ligation of TEF

nodes.description: Persistent communication between the esophagus and trachea is very frequently associated with esophageal atresia.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002575 | Tracheoesophageal fistula | Very frequent (99-80%)

evidence.reference_title: Perioperative management and outcomes of esophageal atresia and tracheoesophageal fistula.
evidence.snippet: The most common anatomy was proximal EA with distal TEF (n=335; 85%)
